chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: This is the story of how I used comics to help me in my role

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: as a caregiver for my mom,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: in a way I couldn't have imagined.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Now, when I was thinking about how to tell that story,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I talked a lot about it with my mom.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: That's because this story is hers.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: My mom's name is Jocelyn, and we're not alike.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She's an optimist.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I worry about all the terrible things that are going to go wrong.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She's impulsive, and she's a little bit of a wimp.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: We're so different that it took me a long time to realize

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: we had something important in common.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: We both respond to challenges by writing stories.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Now, it took me such a long time to notice that we had this in common

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: because the stories we wrote were so different.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She self-published a book of poems and short stories

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: about living with Parkinson's disease over the last 20 years.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I write comics, like this one.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And my comics are about stuff like this.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Now, over the years, my mom and I got used to her physical health challenges.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But a while ago, she started having cognitive challenges, too.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She couldn't write stories anymore,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: and communication between us kept getting harder.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: My mom's doctor didn't get it.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: He asked her questions like,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Well, what city are you in?

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She knows the answer.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: He asked, What year is it?

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Well, she knew that answer, too.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: He just said, You're fine.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But my mom was so worried about me.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But my mom had been having hallucinations.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She sometimes thought she was surrounded by ghostly people.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: The doctor couldn't see it.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But my mom could.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And now, so can you.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I want to tell you about graphic medicine,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: a growing movement that refers to an unlikely partnership

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: between health care and comics.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Now, at the heart of this movement is a kind of a comic called

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: A Graphic Pathography.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: A graphic pathography just means a story about illness

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: that's told in a visual medium.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: This comic's that kind of a story.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: You can see the ghostly hallucinations.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And you're invited to feel empathy for the patient's experience.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: You can share my mom's concern that her symptoms have not been recognized.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: That's one way that words and pictures can work together

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: to tell a health care story.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And this is something anyone can do.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Now, you might be thinking, No, I'm not a comics artist.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But that's OK. You don't have to be.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: A health care story can benefit from very simple pictures.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I'm going to show you how.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But we've got to go back a few years to a time before I drew those pictures

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: that I just shared.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: My mom's condition got worse, and we were in the hospital a lot.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: By this time, hallucinations and early-stage dementia made it hard

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: for her to explain what was going on.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And it was hard for the hospital staff to trust her.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I was constantly hanging around the hospital.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I felt like everything depended on me.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Doctors and nurses came and went, arriving and leaving unexpectedly.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And I felt like I was standing at the side of a highway trying to get

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: the attention of the drivers racing past.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: There was this one evening when I really had to get home to my kids.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But I didn't want to leave the hospital, because my mom had been having

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: this weird symptom.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She was leaning off to the left.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Her head and upper body slumped sideways.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: During the day, she'd slide out of her wheelchair,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: and her leg would get caught in the wheels.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And at night, her inability to straighten out her body

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: made it hard for her to get a good sleep.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Now, the doctor was aware of this, and we'd ruled out the possibility

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: that there was anything dangerous going on, like a stroke.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But still, I didn't want to leave, because the night staff were going to arrive.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And they didn't know my mom.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: This sideways posture didn't have anything to do with the reason

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: she'd actually been admitted to the hospital.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: They might just overlook it.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Or if they did notice it, they might assume this was just her regular posture.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And it really wasn't.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I didn't know what to do.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And that's when it came to me.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: A picture could help.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: So I drew one.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I wrote, Help for Jocelyn.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She leans to the left.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Please support wheelchair and bed with pillows.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I drew a circle around the leg that kept getting injured.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And I drew my mom lying in bed.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And I wrote, This is a comfy sleeping position.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I taped it up on the wall above her bed, and I left.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And suddenly I felt like I didn't need to keep standing at the side of that highway.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: It was as if I just planted a big sign at the side of the road

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: that anyone passing by would see.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And I could go home.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Thanks to that picture, I got a good night's sleep.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And so did my mom.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: When I went back the next morning, I saw that someone had propped up her left arm with a pillow.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: A nurse who I'd never met had seen the picture and known what to do.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: This was the first of so many pictures I drew to help my mom.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And what surprised me was how fast this worked.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I carried pictures like this around with me everywhere I went

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: to pull out whenever I needed them to save me explaining things again.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And I learned that a picture is worth a thousand words that you just don't have time to say.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Then my mom was moved to another part of the hospital,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: and there was a whole new team of staff members who didn't know her.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: So I got ready to start drawing some new pictures.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But then I thought,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: When I drew those first pictures for my mom,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I had made choices about what health care issues to highlight on her behalf.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: At that time, she hadn't had the words to speak for herself.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: So those pictures were just my best guesses about what might help.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But big questions arise when you try to tell someone else's story.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: That kind of collaboration depends on trust.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: So this time, I sat down with my mom, and I asked her what pictures I should draw.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Her answers surprised me.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She said,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Please tell them to call me Jocelyn.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: They don't know I go by my middle name.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She said,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Please tell them I'm left-handed.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And she asked me to draw a food tray on which the items had been placed where her hand could reach them.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She asked me to draw a picture that said,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Please remove lids.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: That's because nerve damage in her hands makes fine motor skills a challenge.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She asked me to draw a picture that said,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Please fill cups halfway.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: A full cup is too heavy.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She asked me to draw a picture that said,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Please tell me your name.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I can't read your name tag.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And she asked me to draw a picture to go on the door of her room,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: so she would know which room was hers.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: These small details were a big deal.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: They gave me insight into challenges I hadn't even been aware of.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Now on the picture of the door of my mom's room,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I drew her face.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I've drawn my mom so many times, I have a way of drawing her face.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But the point isn't that it has to look anything like my mom.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: It could be a circle with two dots for eyes.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: The point is that there's a face.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: There's a person with a voice.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And if you listen to the picture, the voice can be heard.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Because the face can be seen.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: The message matters more.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Because it comes from someone.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: After I'd drawn all those pictures, my mom asked me,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Now draw one more.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Draw me looking healthy.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Draw me walking with my walker.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And label it, Jocelyn's goal.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She said, The staff here are just going to see a sick old lady in the hospital bed.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Someone who's weak and confused.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: It's easy to think that's all I am.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She said, I want them to understand what we're working for.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Sometimes you have to see it to believe it.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: If I'm honest, I have to admit that sometimes I was the one who just saw the sick old lady in the hospital bed.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And trying to capture my mom's goal in a picture helped me believe in it more myself.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: My mom did reach her goal, and a few months later she walked out of the hospital on her own two feet.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She moved to long-term care, and for the first time, her care needs were more managed and predictable.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Now, she did still hallucinate about being surrounded by ghostly people.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But my mom and I have always responded to challenges by writing stories.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And now, we've learned to write stories together.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Like this one.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Here's me asking my mom, How's the writing going?

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And she responds, Not great. Maybe I need a ghostwriter.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I already have the ghost.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Remember my mom's doctor, the one who didn't get it?

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: That comic about him was part of this same story.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: It's a comic my mom and I wrote together for a magazine dedicated to destigmatizing dementia and supporting people impacted by this disease.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: My mom's name appeared in the byline right next to mine.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And this comic was one of the ways we carefully documented her symptoms, which led to her being able to start a new medication that helped with those ghostly hallucinations.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But more than that, this comic let her use her experience to help others whom the magazine could reach.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And besides, isn't it just cool that a medical magazine these days has comics?

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: My mom and I have continued to write comics together.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And she's continued to trust me with sharing the stories of life with dementia and life in long-term care during the pandemic.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I think she's been okay with me sharing these vulnerable moments because she knows I'm not just telling the story of a sick old lady in the hospital bed.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She knows I understand that even though I may be the one drawing the pictures, she's a collaborator with an equal part in the work.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And here's my mom saying, Do not write about that in this comic.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: The reason this all started didn't have anything to do with art or writing or even health care.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: It came from me wanting to help my mom.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And that's the same power you have in your relationships with the people you care for.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: You know their health care needs. You live their stories with them.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Now, I understand you may still feel a bit skeptical about showing up at the doctor's office with a sketchbook,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: but you may be surprised to discover that the people in your health care community are already familiar with graphic medicine,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: the growing movement at the intersection of health care and comics.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: They may already know how a picture can be an amazing time saver or a tool for creating empathy and personal connections.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Just imagine if your new doctor opened your chart and saw pictures that sparked curiosity about the person, not just the symptoms.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: When I looked at all the pictures I'd drawn of my mom, I did see her symptoms.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But I also see my mom. She's there in all the words and pictures that have continued to hold us together.

text: This is the story of how I used comics to help me in my role as a caregiver for my mom, in a way I couldn't have imagined. Now, when I was thinking about how to tell that story, I talked a lot about it with my mom. That's because this story is hers. My mom's name is Jocelyn, and we're not alike. She's an optimist. I worry about all the terrible things that are going to go wrong. She's impulsive, and she's a little bit of a wimp. We're so different that it took me a long time to realize we had something important in common. We both respond to challenges by writing stories. Now, it took me such a long time to notice that we had this in common because the stories we wrote were so different. She self-published a book of poems and short stories about living with Parkinson's disease over the last 20 years. I write comics, like this one. And my comics are about stuff like this. Now, over the years, my mom and I got used to her physical health challenges. But a while ago, she started having cognitive challenges, too. She couldn't write stories anymore, and communication between us kept getting harder. My mom's doctor didn't get it. He asked her questions like, Well, what city are you in? She knows the answer. He asked, What year is it? Well, she knew that answer, too. He just said, You're fine. But my mom was so worried about me. But my mom had been having hallucinations. She sometimes thought she was surrounded by ghostly people. The doctor couldn't see it. But my mom could. And now, so can you. I want to tell you about graphic medicine, a growing movement that refers to an unlikely partnership between health care and comics. Now, at the heart of this movement is a kind of a comic called A Graphic Pathography. A graphic pathography just means a story about illness that's told in a visual medium. This comic's that kind of a story. You can see the ghostly hallucinations. And you're invited to feel empathy for the patient's experience. You can share my mom's concern that her symptoms have not been recognized. That's one way that words and pictures can work together to tell a health care story. And this is something anyone can do. Now, you might be thinking, No, I'm not a comics artist. But that's OK. You don't have to be. A health care story can benefit from very simple pictures. I'm going to show you how. But we've got to go back a few years to a time before I drew those pictures that I just shared. My mom's condition got worse, and we were in the hospital a lot. By this time, hallucinations and early-stage dementia made it hard for her to explain what was going on. And it was hard for the hospital staff to trust her. I was constantly hanging around the hospital. I felt like everything depended on me. Doctors and nurses came and went, arriving and leaving unexpectedly. And I felt like I was standing at the side of a highway trying to get the attention of the drivers racing past. There was this one evening when I really had to get home to my kids. But I didn't want to leave the hospital, because my mom had been having this weird symptom. She was leaning off to the left. Her head and upper body slumped sideways. During the day, she'd slide out of her wheelchair, and her leg would get caught in the wheels. And at night, her inability to straighten out her body made it hard for her to get a good sleep. Now, the doctor was aware of this, and we'd ruled out the possibility that there was anything dangerous going on, like a stroke. But still, I didn't want to leave, because the night staff were going to arrive. And they didn't know my mom. This sideways posture didn't have anything to do with the reason she'd actually been admitted to the hospital. They might just overlook it. Or if they did notice it, they might assume this was just her regular posture. And it really wasn't. I didn't know what to do. And that's when it came to me. A picture could help. So I drew one. I wrote, Help for Jocelyn. She leans to the left. Please support wheelchair and bed with pillows. I drew a circle around the leg that kept getting injured. And I drew my mom lying in bed. And I wrote, This is a comfy sleeping position. I taped it up on the wall above her bed, and I left. And suddenly I felt like I didn't need to keep standing at the side of that highway. It was as if I just planted a big sign at the side of the road that anyone passing by would see. And I could go home. Thanks to that picture, I got a good night's sleep. And so did my mom. When I went back the next morning, I saw that someone had propped up her left arm with a pillow. A nurse who I'd never met had seen the picture and known what to do. This was the first of so many pictures I drew to help my mom. And what surprised me was how fast this worked. I carried pictures like this around with me everywhere I went to pull out whenever I needed them to save me explaining things again. And I learned that a picture is worth a thousand words that you just don't have time to say. Then my mom was moved to another part of the hospital, and there was a whole new team of staff members who didn't know her. So I got ready to start drawing some new pictures. But then I thought, When I drew those first pictures for my mom, I had made choices about what health care issues to highlight on her behalf. At that time, she hadn't had the words to speak for herself. So those pictures were just my best guesses about what might help. But big questions arise when you try to tell someone else's story. That kind of collaboration depends on trust. So this time, I sat down with my mom, and I asked her what pictures I should draw. Her answers surprised me. She said, Please tell them to call me Jocelyn. They don't know I go by my middle name. She said, Please tell them I'm left-handed. And she asked me to draw a food tray on which the items had been placed where her hand could reach them. She asked me to draw a picture that said, Please remove lids. That's because nerve damage in her hands makes fine motor skills a challenge. She asked me to draw a picture that said, Please fill cups halfway. A full cup is too heavy. She asked me to draw a picture that said, Please tell me your name. I can't read your name tag. And she asked me to draw a picture to go on the door of her room, so she would know which room was hers. These small details were a big deal. They gave me insight into challenges I hadn't even been aware of. Now on the picture of the door of my mom's room, I drew her face. I've drawn my mom so many times, I have a way of drawing her face. But the point isn't that it has to look anything like my mom. It could be a circle with two dots for eyes. The point is that there's a face. There's a person with a voice. And if you listen to the picture, the voice can be heard. Because the face can be seen. The message matters more. Because it comes from someone. After I'd drawn all those pictures, my mom asked me, Now draw one more. Draw me looking healthy. Draw me walking with my walker. And label it, Jocelyn's goal. She said, The staff here are just going to see a sick old lady in the hospital bed. Someone who's weak and confused. It's easy to think that's all I am. She said, I want them to understand what we're working for. Sometimes you have to see it to believe it. If I'm honest, I have to admit that sometimes I was the one who just saw the sick old lady in the hospital bed. And trying to capture my mom's goal in a picture helped me believe in it more myself. My mom did reach her goal, and a few months later she walked out of the hospital on her own two feet. She moved to long-term care, and for the first time, her care needs were more managed and predictable. Now, she did still hallucinate about being surrounded by ghostly people. But my mom and I have always responded to challenges by writing stories. And now, we've learned to write stories together. Like this one. Here's me asking my mom, How's the writing going? And she responds, Not great. Maybe I need a ghostwriter. I already have the ghost. Remember my mom's doctor, the one who didn't get it? That comic about him was part of this same story. It's a comic my mom and I wrote together for a magazine dedicated to destigmatizing dementia and supporting people impacted by this disease. My mom's name appeared in the byline right next to mine. And this comic was one of the ways we carefully documented her symptoms, which led to her being able to start a new medication that helped with those ghostly hallucinations. But more than that, this comic let her use her experience to help others whom the magazine could reach. And besides, isn't it just cool that a medical magazine these days has comics? My mom and I have continued to write comics together. And she's continued to trust me with sharing the stories of life with dementia and life in long-term care during the pandemic. I think she's been okay with me sharing these vulnerable moments because she knows I'm not just telling the story of a sick old lady in the hospital bed. She knows I understand that even though I may be the one drawing the pictures, she's a collaborator with an equal part in the work. And here's my mom saying, Do not write about that in this comic. The reason this all started didn't have anything to do with art or writing or even health care. It came from me wanting to help my mom. And that's the same power you have in your relationships with the people you care for. You know their health care needs. You live their stories with them. Now, I understand you may still feel a bit skeptical about showing up at the doctor's office with a sketchbook, but you may be surprised to discover that the people in your health care community are already familiar with graphic medicine, the growing movement at the intersection of health care and comics. They may already know how a picture can be an amazing time saver or a tool for creating empathy and personal connections. Just imagine if your new doctor opened your chart and saw pictures that sparked curiosity about the person, not just the symptoms. When I looked at all the pictures I'd drawn of my mom, I did see her symptoms. But I also see my mom. She's there in all the words and pictures that have continued to hold us together.
